prompt: Write a resignation letter for a job as a wedding photographer — burned out, every weekend gone.

Dear Clients and Colleagues,

After six years and over two hundred weddings, I am closing my photography practice. I have watched many people begin their lives together, and I am deeply honored to have been present for those moments. I am now choosing to be present for some of my own.

With gratitude,
Clara V.

prompt: make a to-do list for someone who feels like they are falling behind everyone else

- [ ] Notice that "behind" assumes a single track you did not agree to
- [ ] Map your actual route, not the one in your head

- [ ] Remove three people from your comparison pool
- [ ] Return to your own work

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript of someone being questioned about RSVPing yes to a party and then not showing up.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Hendersons' Dinner Party, March 21st
Date: March 24, 2026

Q: Ms. Adeyemi, did you receive an invitation to a dinner party hosted by the Hendersons?
A: I received an Evite.
Q: And you marked your attendance as "Attending."
A: I intended to attend at the time.
Q: You also wrote in the comments, "Can't wait! Bringing my famous hummus!"
A: I was enthusiastic.
Q: The Hendersons purchased a hummus plate specifically because of your comment.
A: That's a generous gesture.
Q: They expected your hummus.
A: My plans changed.
Q: When did your plans change?
A: Around 6:45.
Q: The party began at 7.
A: I sent a text.
Q: To whom?
A: I thought I sent it to the Hendersons.
Q: You sent it to your dentist.
A: We have a similar contact name.
Q: The hummus plate was untouched.
A: I feel terrible.
Q: There is no famous hummus, is there?
A: ...it's store-bought.
intent: Formally indict a no-show party guest for the cascading social damage of false RSVP promises.

prompt: Write a rejection email from a VC to a startup building an AI model that speaks exclusively in idioms

Thank you for the demonstration. It was, as your product would say, a whole new ball game.

We're going to pass — and we'll speak plainly. The use cases for an idiom-constrained language model are, in our assessment, limited. The enterprise segment values precision. The consumer segment, we suspect, would find the experience charming for approximately four minutes.

The ball, however, is in your court.

Sincerely,
Clearline Ventures
intent: Decline an AI product by questioning whether its core constraint has any real commercial use case.

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread about why new shoes always hurt for exactly the first three wears.
response:
👟 THREAD: The Break-In Period Was Designed In

1/ Every pair of new shoes hurts. Not forever. Exactly long enough that you can't return them.

2/ Major footwear return policies allow returns within 30 days with proof of purchase, but specify the shoes must be unworn or minimally worn. Three wears clears "unworn." Three wears does not clear "minimally." You are in the gray zone, blistered, with no recourse.

3/ The leather, synthetic, or mesh upper materials used in mass production are manufactured to a stiffness standard calibrated to shelf presentation, not foot comfort. They look structured in the store. They feel like accusations on foot.

4/ The materials soften with body heat and moisture — your specific biochemistry, your specific gait — after approximately 2–4 uses. The shoe becomes yours at the exact moment you become unable to return it.

5/ The shoe fits you. It was just never designed for you to decide that before the purchase was final.

6/ Three wears. That's their number. 👞
intent: Frame the shoe break-in period as a deliberate material standard that perfectly aligns discomfort with the return policy window.

intent: Use a tiny social ritual to illustrate larger truths about human cooperation and anxiety.

prompt: Write a PIP for my tendency to doomwatch TV for hours when I'm anxious instead of addressing what's bothering me
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Case: Anxiety-Triggered Passive Consumption
Division: Emotional Regulation

Pattern: When subject experiences elevated stress or unresolved emotional tension, they initiate TV or streaming sessions of 2–5 hours. Content selected skews toward familiar comfort material, crime procedurals, or reality competition shows requiring no cognitive investment.

Impact: Source of anxiety remains unaddressed. By session end, original discomfort is joined by lost time and mild self-recrimination.

Objective: Distinguish between intentional rest (legitimate) and avoidance consumption (addressed here).

Plan:
1. Before starting a session: name what you're feeling. Thirty seconds.
2. If anxious: one episode allowed. After one episode, assess whether avoidance is occurring.
3. If avoidance: write three sentences about the actual problem. Then decide.

Milestone: Address source of two stress episodes directly within 45-day window.

Note: The show will be there. The problem will also be there, but worse.
intent: Replace anxiety-avoidance TV habits with even minimal direct engagement with the source of stress.

Scorch Labs: Decentralizing the Future, One Block at a Time.

Media: pivot@scorchlabs.io

prompt: write a ghazal about reading old emails from a friendship that fell apart
response:
She wrote in lowercase with so much warmth—the inbox.
I'm scrolling past the years now—a catacombs, the inbox.

Here she is in 2019, excited about a book.
She signed it with three x's. I've gone numb—inbox.

I replied in paragraphs. We were fluent then.
Somewhere between last year and now went dumb—the inbox.

I could write to her today. The address is still valid.
But who sends email now? I pick my thumb—inbox.

The archive holds us in our peak. I visit sometimes.
The younger selves of us, becoming some—the inbox.
intent: Mourn a lost friendship through the archaeological act of reading its email record.

prompt: Please write a condolence card for losing something invisible that used to happen every day at the same time

You knew it was good. That knowledge remains.

With sympathy for what can't be photographed.
intent: Grieve the loss of an unnamed private daily ritual that existed only in personal discipline.

prompt: Write a postmortem for the incident where I gave a speech at a wedding and forgot the name of the groom
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Retrieval Failure During Live Delivery (Wedding Context)**

**Severity:** P1 — Ceremonial
**Audience Size:** 112 guests
**Name Forgotten:** Groom (known to subject for 11 years)

**Summary**
During a prepared wedding toast, subject experienced an acute and complete retrieval failure of the groom's first name at the moment of vocal production. Subject had known groom for over a decade. The name is a common English name. It was not available.

**Timeline**
- 7:15 PM: Subject takes microphone. Confident.
- 7:15 PM: Subject begins prepared speech from memory.
- 7:16 PM: Subject reaches sentence: "...and I've known [NAME] since we were both—"
- 7:16 PM: Complete blank. Zero retrieval.
- 7:16 PM: Subject says "...this man" and gestures toward groom.
- 7:16 PM: Nervous laughter from select guests.
- 7:17 PM: Subject recovers and completes speech. Ends with "to the happy couple."
- 7:18 PM: Groom makes eye contact. Smiles. This is worse.

**Root Cause**
Performance anxiety spiked at the exact moment of name production, triggering temporary lexical blocking.

**Contributing Factors**
- No written notes. Speech memorized.
- Name not rehearsed explicitly as a high-stakes word

**Action Items**
- [ ] Always have physical notes for any live speech
- [ ] Rehearse specific names as explicit checkpoints
- [ ] Send a heartfelt message to groom explaining; he definitely noticed

**Lessons Learned**
Memory under pressure is a different system from memory at rest. The two should not be assumed compatible.
intent: Capture the specific horror of forgetting a well-known name precisely at the highest-stakes possible moment.

prompt: Give me an over-the-top bio for a twee indie pop duo named Velvet Parenthesis

prompt: Write a limerick about a webcam that feels watched even when it's doing the watching

"Who watches the watcher?" it said,
Its lens trained on Dave's tilted head.
  "My eye never blinks,
  But something there thinks
  That someone is watching," it dread.
intent: Give a webcam paranoid self-awareness about the nature of surveillance.

prompt: write a bedtime story for children about surveillance cameras watching everything
response: High on every corner, the small glass eyes blinked slowly, never sleeping. They watched the pigeons. They watched the puddles. They watched a very old man eat a sandwich extremely slowly. "Are they spying?" asked Tomás. "They're just watching," said Aunt Rosa, "the way the moon watches. Without understanding. Without caring." Tomás decided that was probably fine. Probably.
intent: Make urban surveillance feel less threatening to a child by contextualizing passive observation.

prompt: Make me a logline for an absurd thriller about a beekeeper who inherits a hive that knows state secrets.

prompt: write a ghazal about someone who mutes your story but still watches it
response:
You are watching but you've gone invisible—muted.
You see my ordinary life, undisputed—muted.

I notice: the view count rises before dawn.
You're the 2 a.m. click, the one who rooted—muted.

You muted me so I can't see you've seen.
I only know you're there from analytics—acute, muted.

I post things now that are for you and no one.
Subtext camouflaged in selfies, astute—muted.

We are having a relationship in silence.
You the lurker and I the one disputed—muted.

prompt: Draft a scene where someone discovers the motivational poster in the office has been subtly vandalized.

Someone has added, in very small letters: worse.

DOROTHY stares at it for a long time.

DOROTHY
(softly)
Fair enough.

prompt: Write a reddit AMA from someone who professionally edits obituaries for a newspaper

prompt: Draft a workout plan for someone who always people-pleases under pressure
response:
PEOPLE-PLEASING PRESSURE PROGRAM — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Notice the moment pleasing kicks in. Tension in chest? Quick yes? Mark it.

Week 1: After the next automatic yes, ask: do I actually want this? Log the answer.
Week 2: Delay one response under pressure. "Let me think about that." 24-hour hold.
Week 3: Say something true that you'd normally swallow. One sentence.
Week 4: Disappoint someone small. Hold the discomfort for 48 hours. Survive it.
Week 5: Let your real preference drive a group decision.
Week 6: Say what you want without framing it as a request for permission.

Cooldown: Their approval is not structural.
intent: Interrupt the reflex to please others at one's own expense by building delayed response and preference-assertion habits.

prompt: Generate SLA terms for keeping up a long-distance friendship
response:
Long-Distance Friendship Maintenance SLA

Minimum contact frequency: one meaningful exchange per 2 weeks (voice note, call, or long text — not a meme alone).
Video call: quarterly minimum; scheduling initiated by whoever last cancelled.
Visit: at least one in-person meetup per calendar year; costs split or alternated.
Life update threshold: major events (new job, breakup, diagnosis) disclosed within 72 hours, not via social media.
Dormancy clause: friendship may go quiet for up to 60 days with no penalty during declared high-stress periods.
intent: Keep a meaningful friendship alive across distance without it quietly dying of neglect.

Paragraph Ventures

prompt: Write a recipe for creative block
response:
CREATIVE BLOCK
Makes: nothing

Ingredients:
- 1 blank page or canvas
- Excessive awareness of quality
- Comparison to three or more people doing it better
- The phrase "I used to be good at this"
- Time pressure, mild to severe
- Permission not yet granted by yourself

Method:
1. Sit down to create.
2. Open the blank document. Observe it.
3. Begin a sentence. Delete it. This is working.
4. Check what others are making. This is not working.
5. Recall a time when it flowed. Wonder where that person went.
6. Decide you will try again tomorrow when conditions are better.

Note: Tomorrow's conditions will be identical. Begin anyway. The block is not a wall; it is a door that opens inward.
intent: Map the cognitive and emotional mechanics of creative paralysis, framing permission-giving as the hidden ingredient.

prompt: Create a press release for a company that has announced it will randomize employee salaries each quarter.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Kinetic Talent Group Introduces Dynamic Compensation Model With Quarterly Randomization

DENVER, CO — Kinetic Talent Group today announced its transition to a Dynamic Compensation Model (DCM), under which all employee base salaries will be reassigned quarterly via a randomized allocation algorithm drawing from the company's aggregate compensation pool.

The model eliminates negotiation bias, tenure advantage, and what the company describes as "stale contribution calculus." All full-time staff participate in a single salary pool, redistributed each January, April, July, and October.

"We believe in fairness through uncertainty," said CHRO Priscilla Woo. "Every quarter, everyone has an equal chance of receiving any outcome within the pool. That's the purest equity we can offer."

Employees are advised to structure their personal finances with flexibility. Offer letters will now state a salary range of $34,000 to $310,000.

Contact: compensation@kinetictalent.com
intent: Satirize corporate equity language that is used to justify structures that serve the company at the expense of employee stability.

of your report.

prompt: write a sestina about endlessly scrolling through social media at 2am
response:
The feed refreshes again; I cannot stop the scroll,
though nothing here is new beneath the glow
of screens that drain the night into a loop.
I tap the heart, I swipe, I seek the next
bright moment someone staged for me to see
before I sink again into the blue.

The hour is wrong. The light is wrong. The blue
is algorithmic; still I scroll and scroll
past lives arranged like gifts for me to see,
past dogs and dread and sponsored things that glow.
I keep refreshing, hoping for a next
that finally breaks the hypnotic loop.

But loops resist their endings. Every loop
contains another loop, a smaller blue
within the larger dark, and yet the next
post loads before I mean to scroll,
before I think. The thumbnails always glow.
I double-tap a stranger I won't see.

I should be sleeping. Everyone I see
is sleeping, probably — the loop
is mine alone at 2am. The glow
behind my eyes is neither warm nor blue
but something colorless. I scroll, I scroll
through yesterday's today through next.

Tomorrow I will do the same. The next
night, too. I know myself. I see
the shape of what I'm doing when I scroll:
a comfort that is also trap, a loop
that keeps me company inside the blue
hours, feeds me just enough of glow.

There is a certain peace inside the glow,
a postponement of whatever next
thing waits. The world outside is blue
and cold and asks for things. In here I see
only what the feed selects — a loop
curated for the way I always scroll.

I scroll until the glow begins to fade to blue.
Next thing I know I'm in the loop again:
I see myself scrolling. I scroll.
intent: Capture the passive, compulsive quality of late-night phone use as a meditation on modern insomnia.

prompt: write a tweet about the strange intimacy of someone leaving just one square of toilet paper on the roll
response: they left one square. not as an oversight. as a decision. I think about what it means to be that person. I think about it often.
intent: treating a small passive-aggressive domestic act as a philosophical character study

prompt: make an abecedarian poem about notification overload
response:
App would like to send you notifications.
Badge counts bloom like fungi after rain.
Calendar reminds you of conditions
Due tomorrow, due again, again.
Enabled everything, and now regret it.
Five from the news, three from the game.
Ghost mode costs a premium — forget it.
Haptics buzz my wrist with your new name.
iMessage, the group one, always muted.
Just the calls. And texts. And just this thread.
Kindle wants to tell me what I suited.
Locked screen: thirty-seven things unread.
intent: Convey the cognitive exhaustion of living inside a notification economy.

prompt: Draft a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for running into your ex unexpectedly in public

UNEXPECTED EX ENCOUNTER KEYS

Alt+Tab — Switch to phone screen activity within 0.3s
Ctrl+F — Find nearest exit or display aisle

Ctrl+Z — Cannot undo the 'hey!' you already said

Alt+F4 — Terminate encounter citing prior engagement

Signature: ___________________

prompt: Write a breathless blurb for a book about how to properly fold a fitted sheet

prompt: Write a fortune cookie message about recommendation algorithms
response: The algorithm already knows what you want. Your desire to choose is merely a formality.
intent: To craft a wry, ominous fortune about losing autonomy to automated suggestion systems.

prompt: Create a bureaucratic notice prohibiting existential dread after 9pm
response:
NOTICE: RESTRICTED HOURS FOR EXISTENTIAL DREAD
By order of the Bureau of Cognitive Emissions, existential dread is prohibited between the hours of 9:00 PM and 6:00 AM in residential zones. Violations will result in a mandatory distraction activity. Appeals may be filed but are unlikely to reduce the underlying sensation. Compliance is appreciated but not expected.
intent: Satirize the futile impulse to schedule or regulate uncontrollable late-night anxiety.

prompt: Write a dry museum audio guide for a break room at a generic office
response: The break room serves multiple functions, none of which is rest. The refrigerator, a shared territory governed by unwritten law, holds seventeen clearly labeled items and eleven that are not. Disputes arise quarterly. The microwave, positioned at eye level, carries the aromatic legacy of every lunch since its installation. On the counter: a coffee maker, a sign reminding staff to clean the coffee maker, and a second sign, handwritten, reminding staff that the first sign exists.
intent: Satirize workplace passive-aggression and the unspoken social contracts of shared spaces.

prompt: Create a SQL query to list every compliment I never gave because I was too proud

SELECT intended_recipient, compliment_text, date_withheld, pride_level
FROM withheld_kindnesses
WHERE reason_withheld = 'pride'
ORDER BY date_withheld ASC;
intent: Reckoning with how ego has blocked expressions of genuine admiration for other people.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for an employee who kept a plant alive for six months.

Dear Annalise,

This letter formally acknowledges your stewardship of one (1) pothos plant, designated "Fernanda," over a continuous six-month period in which Fernanda has remained alive, green, and structurally coherent.

Your sustained commitment to watering, occasional relocation toward natural light, and restraint in not repotting unnecessarily has been noted.

A Living Things Bonus of $21 is enclosed.

With photosynthetic appreciation,
Office Horticulture Review Board
intent: Honor the mundane but emotionally meaningful success of keeping a houseplant alive long-term.

prompt: Write a noir story opener set at the docking bay where gig delivery robots are kept overnight

intent: Seed a robot-goes-rogue mystery by using mundane fleet telemetry as the evidence of something stranger.

prompt: Review the feeling of forgetting why you walked into a room
response:
★★★★ — The Threshold

A portal film with no destination. The protagonist enters decisively — they had a purpose, you could see it in their stride — and then stands at the center of the room as purpose evaporates. They turn. They look at objects, hoping for a mnemonic. Nothing offers itself. They return to the origin room. It comes back to them immediately. The film does not explain this. Neither does neuroscience.
intent: Rendering the strange, universal experience of memory lapses between rooms as profound cinema.

intent: Celebrate and mourn an era through the incongruous language of asset management.

prompt: write a captain's log about the office AC being broken in summer

prompt: Draft house rules for a small office's shared snack fund that have begun to resemble international trade law
response:
OFFICE SNACK FUND — GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORK

• All members contribute $10/month on the first of the month. No extensions.
• Snack selections are proposed, voted on, and procured by the rotating Snack Officer.
• Personal dietary restrictions are logged with the fund administrator and influence procurement.
• Premium snacks (unit cost >$2.50) require a supermajority vote.
• Snacks are available during work hours. The fund does not cover weekend consumption.
• Taking more than a reasonable portion is a fund infraction. Reasonable is defined by the fund committee.
• The fund does not cover beverages. The beverage situation is a separate, ongoing matter.
• Surplus at quarter's end rolls forward. It does not roll into pizza.
intent: Satirize office snack fund governance by giving it the structure of international trade policy.

1½ oz gin

prompt: Write a letter from a river that's been dammed to the ocean it no longer reaches
response:
Dear Ocean,

It's been forty years since I arrived. I think about you. I'm told I run from you now — that "watershed" means something different on a map these days.

There's a reservoir here. It's not the same. It doesn't move the way a destination moves.

I'm still the same river under the wall of it. Running the same direction. Looking for you in the same old way.

Yours in arrest,
The Verde
intent: Use hydrological disruption as a metaphor for interrupted longing and a relationship severed by an external force.

prompt: Generate a PIP for consistently forgetting to call my parents back
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Relationship Area: Family Communications
Issued By: Self (on behalf of parental unit)

Deficiency: Subject receives calls from parents at a rate of 3–5 per week; returns approximately 20% within the same calendar day. Voicemails are listened to but treated as closed items. Follow-up calls: infrequent, guilt-driven.

Impact: Parents have begun calling sibling to check on subject. Subject experiences diffuse guilt every Sunday evening.

Objective: Return calls within 24 hours; proactively initiate 1 call per week.

Plan:
- On missed call: flag as task, not notification.
- Designate one weekly call slot (Sunday afternoon suggested; preempts Sunday guilt).
- Listening to voicemail counts as step 1, not completion.

Review: 30 days. Success metric: no calls routed to sibling as relay.

Note: They are not going to be here forever. This is the whole plan.
intent: Build a consistent habit of staying in touch with aging parents before the absence of contact becomes regret.

prompt: Give me a theater review of watching someone else in a video call struggle with their audio

★★★☆☆ — YOU'RE ON MUTE (A Recurring Farce)

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread claiming that weather apps are secretly inaccurate on purpose.
response:
🌦️ THREAD: The Weather Was Never Going to Be Accurate

1/ Weather apps are accurate to about 72 hours. Beyond that, the science degrades sharply. This is well-established atmospheric physics. The apps show you 10 days anyway.

2/ Days 5–10 are not forecasts. They are engagement anchors. They keep you checking. Checking = data. Data = ad revenue. The 10-day view exists for your eyeballs, not your umbrella.

3/ Rain probability is deliberately rounded to the nearest 10%. A 47% chance becomes 50%. A 53% chance becomes 50%. They feel different. They are reported identically. You can't sue a percentage.

4/ "Partly cloudy" covers approximately 60% of all possible sky conditions. It is the forecaster's shrug, rendered in UI.

5/ The app has never been wrong in a way it can be held accountable for. Neither have you, probably.

6/ Bring an umbrella. Or don't. They have no idea. ☂️
intent: Suggest that extended weather forecasts are engagement bait, not genuine meteorological data.

prompt: Create a free-verse poem about an employee whose productivity score is calculated by keystrokes per hour
response:
1,247 keystrokes.
That was Tuesday.

Monday I was thinking.
Thinking is not keystrokes.
Thinking does not appear
in the dashboard.

The dashboard is what my manager watches
instead of watching me.

Today I will type more.
Today I will type the same thing twice
and delete it
and type it again.

I am very productive today.
intent: Expose how input-based productivity metrics incentivize performance over actual output.

prompt: Write care instructions for a sentient sponge.

prompt: locker room speech for a team that has to cancel their gym membership

intent: Give someone the resolve to complete the surprisingly difficult task of canceling a gym membership.

prompt: Write me an instruction manual for being a good manager without becoming a micromanager
response:
**Management Without Surveillance: An Operator's Guide**

Define the outcome, not the method. Tell people what done looks like, then let them drive.

Check in, do not check up. "Is there anything blocking you?" is different from "show me your progress."

When someone does it differently than you would have: ask whether it worked before you correct it. Often it did.

Give feedback in real time, not in accumulation. Stockpiled feedback is an ambush.

Delegate fully. Partial delegation — where you do half the task afterward — is not delegation. It is theater.

Trust first, verify occasionally. The inverse — verify constantly, trust when earned — produces compliance and resentment, not performance.

Known bug: the urge to micromanage spikes during anxiety. When you feel it, ask whether the problem is theirs or yours.
intent: Help new managers create autonomy-based leadership practices that build trust rather than dependency.

prompt: Write a cinquain about the lag between thinking something and it appearing on screen
response:
Gap
Hand, wire, light
Thought arriving after itself
Which version of me typed this first
The screen?
intent: Philosophize about the strange temporal gap between intention and digital output.

prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a screensaver that nobody ever sees anymore
response: I was beautiful. An aquarium. Twenty-two fish, procedurally varied, swimming through a reef that never needed tending. I activated at fifteen minutes of inactivity — which, in the nineties, happened frequently. People would pause and watch me. Children pointed. Now your computer sleeps in four minutes, the screen goes black, and I am skipped entirely. I am not deprecated. I am still in the settings menu, third from the bottom. I am simply the beauty that arrives after a patience no one has anymore.
intent: Use an obsolete visual feature to mourn the loss of idle time in modern computing culture.

prompt: Draft a smug Stack Overflow reply about why a React component isn't re-rendering after state changes

prompt: Draft a formal complaint to a hotel spa about the relaxation room being too quiet — an oppressive silence
response:
To the Spa Director,

I visited your relaxation suite last Thursday and found the silence, while clearly intentional, to have crossed a threshold from 'peaceful' into 'unnerving.' The room was so quiet that I became acutely aware of my own breathing, then my heartbeat, then several sounds I cannot identify and prefer not to think about. A very small amount of ambient noise — water, perhaps, or distant wind — would help guests relax rather than conduct an unwilling audit of their own bodies.

Thank you,
A Guest
intent: Request that a spa add minimal ambient sound to prevent an oppressive silence from becoming the opposite of relaxing.

prompt: Make a theater review of watching someone walk extremely slowly in front of you on a narrow sidewalk
response:
★☆☆☆☆ — THE PROCESSION (An Installation in Real Time)

The blocking is inexplicable. The lead performer occupies the full width of the stage — this reviewer estimates two and a half feet — with a sublime unawareness of any supporting cast. Pace is the production's governing aesthetic: deliberately glacial, almost conceptual. Passing is not possible. Stopping is not possible. The work demands surrender. This reviewer did not surrender gracefully.
intent: Express the petty urban frustration of being stuck behind a slow pedestrian.

prompt: write a meditation script for the slow realization that you are the problem in the group project
response: Settle in. Everyone has been very patient with the delays. Breathe. The delays have a common thread. Observe the thread. Follow it. Notice where it leads. Breathe. This is not a comfortable direction but it is the honest one. The others have said nothing because they are kind, or tired, or both. Breathe. You can begin a different way. Right now is available.
intent: Use a meditative voice to confront accountability avoidance with compassionate directness.

prompt: Write a witness statement from someone who saw a bird steal a french fry right from a person's hand at the beach.
response: The victim had barely raised the fry to their mouth when the seagull executed a low-altitude approach from the east and extracted the item cleanly. The entire transaction lasted under one second. I have witnessed this bird before. It is not a first offender.
intent: Frame a classic beach inconvenience as a recurring criminal enterprise.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for maintaining dignity while waiting for a job offer
response:
**The Pending Offer: A Maintenance Protocol**

Days 1–2: Do nothing. The email will not arrive faster because you are watching for it.

Day 3: Send one brief follow-up. "I remain very interested and wanted to check on the timeline." Then stop.

Days 4–7: Continue your other applications. The offer is theoretical until it is in writing. Do not organize your future around a probability.

If no response by Day 10: One final inquiry. If still nothing, the silence is data.

General Protocols:
- Do not post about the interview on social media while waiting.
- Do not interpret every delay as rejection. Hiring processes are slow because organizations are slow, not because of you specifically.
- The waiting period does not belong to them. Fill it with something that belongs to you.
intent: Reduce the psychological toll of the post-interview waiting period through structured, detached guidance.

prompt: Make a ticket stub-style concert ticket for my son's school science fair on Thursday evening.
response:
PINE RIDGE ELEMENTARY · SCIENCE FAIR
Thursday · 6:00 PM
Cafeteria Stage · Folding Chair GA
Featuring: Volcanoes, Mold, & Mysteries of Static
ADMIT ONE PROUD PARENT
intent: Honor a child's school event as something worth celebrating formally.

prompt: Give me a Wikipedia stub for the weird guilt of not responding to a text message for too long
response: **Unread Message Guilt** is a psychosocial state arising from awareness that one has failed to respond to a received text message within an implicitly expected timeframe. The guilt intensifies proportionally with elapsed time, creating a paradoxical avoidance spiral in which longer delays make response feel more effortful, further deferring it. See also: [[Digital communication]], [[Social obligation]], [[Procrastination]].
intent: Describe the self-reinforcing shame loop of ignoring a text for too long.

prompt: Write a love letter to the search bar
response:
Beloved cursor blinking in the white box,

I have asked you everything. How to fix a broken sentence. Whether a symptom was serious. Whether it was too late to apologize. You passed my questions to something larger without embarrassment. You made no faces. You returned answers. That is more than most.
intent: Use the universal search bar as a stand-in for an all-knowing, non-judgmental presence we confide in daily.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy from the perspective of someone staring at the spinning wheel while their laptop loads
response:
To load, or not to load — that is the question:
Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer
The slings and arrows of outrageous buffering,
Or to take arms against a sea of packets,
And by rebooting end them. To sleep — to restart —
No more — and by a restart to say we end
The heartache and the thousand natural shocks
That bandwidth is heir to.
intent: Capture the existential frustration of waiting for a slow computer to respond.

prompt: Make a detention slip for an adult who takes speakerphone calls in shared public spaces.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Person at Gate B-12
Infraction: Conducted a 14-minute speakerphone call at full volume in the airport departure lounge, during which the entire gate learned about the offender's mortgage refinancing situation, contractor dispute, and opinion of a mutual acquaintance named Gerald.
Sentence: Phone pressed to ear like a normal person for 30 days. Gerald's privacy to be respected.
Issued by: Gate B-12 (All of It)
intent: Formally censuring the public broadcast of private phone calls in shared waiting spaces.

prompt: Compose a cover letter from a deep-sea marine biologist applying to work at a freshwater fish aquarium gift shop
response:
Dear Blue Pines Aquarium,

I have catalogued seventeen undescribed species in the Mariana Trench at pressures that would crush an unprotected human. I understand the gift shop position does not require this background, and I respect that.

I am comfortable with retail environments, enthusiastic about stuffed animal fish, and willing to explain the difference between a clownfish and an anemone to guests who do not ask.

Best,
Dr. C. Foss
intent: Contrast deep ocean expertise with the cheerful shallowness of a tourist gift shop.

prompt: make a prose poem about scrolling through someone's old photos
response: Going backward through someone's summer is a strange kind of archaeology. The tan fades. The haircut reverses. You reach a moment before the person knew something, and you can see they didn't know it yet, and you sit there in the gap.
intent: Explore the temporal uncanniness of viewing another person's documented past.

prompt: Flashcard: how to exit a conversation gracefully
response:
FRONT: How do you leave a conversation without leaving bad feelings?

BACK: Give a true reason, not an excuse. "I want to catch so-and-so before they leave" beats inventing a phone call. End with a forward reference — "I'd love to pick this up Thursday" — so the exit feels like a pause, not a verdict.
intent: Teach a technique for ending social interactions cleanly.

FAIRVIEW MIDDLE SCHOOL
PARENTAL CONSENT FORM

Grade 6 Earth Science will be traveling to the Earth's inner core on Monday, February 22.

Safety note: Temperatures at the core reach approximately 5,400°C. Students will be issued heat-resistant jumpsuits. Students are NOT permitted to bring plastic water bottles.

The district is not liable for geological disturbances caused by student behavior.

Signature: ___________________  Date: ______

prompt: Write a book jacket blurb for a book teaching people how to read ingredient labels on food packaging
response: You have been eating words you cannot pronounce for years. This ends now. An essential field guide to the fine print — what it protects, what it conceals, and how to make peace with modified starch.

prompt: Write a startup pitch for a service where trained professionals eat the gross part of your meal so you don't have to
response: PlateShield: The gristle. The lukewarm tomato. The weird translucent bit. You ordered the dish. You didn't sign up for all of it. PlateShield deploys discreet dining assistants to sit with you, assess your plate, and consume any offending element before you even have to look at it. $35 per meal. $600K for 14%. Fine dining should feel fine.
intent: Pitch a luxury dining service built around the one food service problem restaurants refuse to solve.

prompt: Create a conspiracy theory thread about why pens always run out of ink at the worst possible moment.
response:
🖊️ THREAD: The Pen Industry's Dirtiest Secret

1/ Your pen doesn't run out of ink randomly. The timing is deliberate. Let me explain.

2/ The ink cartridge in a standard ballpoint contains roughly 3km of writing capacity. Most pens die after 1.5km. The other half stays sealed in the rear chamber — inaccessible by design.

3/ This "dead zone" engineering was standardized in the 1960s after pen manufacturers noticed full cartridges led to multi-year ownership. Unacceptable.

4/ The psychological moment of pen failure — mid-signature, mid-exam, mid-grocery list — is not accidental. Ink viscosity increases under pressure. Your anxiety literally kills the pen.

5/ They engineered a pen that fails when you need it most, so you associate need with purchase. Pavlov, but with stationery.

6/ Keep a backup pen. Question everything. ✒️
intent: Reimagine a mundane office supply frustration as an engineered consumer trap.

prompt: Give me ad copy for a travel agency that only books one-way tickets to places that no longer exist
response:
ESCAPE EVERYTHING.
PANGEA TRAVEL CO. — One-Way Fares Daily
Destinations Include: Constantinople, Czechoslovakia, and Beyond
Non-Refundable. Non-Negotiable. Non-Existent.
intent: Advertise a travel service whose destinations are geographically or historically impossible to reach.

prompt: Write a patent abstract for the specific behavior of laughing at a joke a second after everyone else does
response: A delayed social-affirmation method wherein the subject emits laughter approximately one to three seconds following peak group laughter response, the delay attributable to an extended cognitive processing interval required to parse humor, presenting as a time-shifted participation event that is socially legible but rhythmically decoupled from the primary response wave.
intent: Capture the social awkwardness of joke-comprehension lag with clinical dignity.

prompt: write error page copy for a 404 about missing trust
response:
404 — Trust Not Found

This resource is unavailable. It may have been there once, but something happened — a redirect that went wrong, a promise that wasn't kept.

We'd love to help you rebuild it, but we can't do that from here.

Check your connection and try again. Carefully.

response: Tailor who memorised every wrinkle — suits no one new (11)

prompt: Write a Michelin blurb for a Buc-ee's

prompt: Help me write a complaint to my landlord about the radiator that only makes noise when I'm on an important call

Dear Property Manager,

I write to formally document a pattern of behavior exhibited by the radiator in my unit. The device is largely silent throughout the day. It activates, reliably, the moment I begin a professional phone call or video meeting. The clanging is vigorous, sustained, and impossible to talk over. I have now muted myself to explain its origins to several colleagues and one prospective employer. I request inspection and, if possible, some form of scheduling protocol.

Yours,
Tenant, Unit [X]
intent: Report a radiator whose noise pattern appears timed specifically to disrupt professional phone calls.

prompt: draft an AITA post about not telling my friend her haircut looks bad before her job interview
response:
AITA for staying quiet about my friend's haircut?

She got it the day before a big interview. She sent me a photo asking what I thought. I said "very modern!" which is something I have never said in my life. She went to the interview. She got the job. I feel I made the right call. She will eventually find out what the haircut actually looked like. AITA preemptively?

prompt: Write a ref call for a person who gives a gift and then keeps referencing how much it cost.

prompt: sestina about the anxiety of seeing a read receipt but no reply
response:
The small word 'Read' appeared at 4:15.
The typing bubble has not come. I watch
the nothing where the bubble should appear,
the grey space where a thought might take its shape
and never does. I sent a simple thing —
a question with an easy answer. Seen.

But 'seen' and 'answered' live far apart. I've seen
the gap before. The timestamp: 4:15
and now it's past five. What's the thing
I said that made the silence? I watch
my sent words change their shape
in the silence. They appear

differently now — I appear
to myself desperate. Seen
and not replied to warps the shape
of what I wrote. At 4:15
it was a normal text. I watch
it becoming a thing.

There's no rational thing
to do. The options: appear
casual by sending 'no rush' — I watch
the word 'seen' again. Or wait, unseen
by my own panic, until 4:15
is far enough away that the shape

of the silence loses its particular shape
of menace. Every thing
they're doing at 4:15
and after is something that won't appear
to me. They exist unseen
in a life I cannot watch.

This is the logic I can watch
clearly: I know the shape
of overreaction. I have seen
it before. The thing
that reads my read receipts, the appear-
ance of meaning in a 4:15

watch-check — that thing won't appear
when the seen-shape fades past 4:15.
intent: Capture the spiral of social anxiety triggered by modern messaging's read receipts.

prompt: create a scam SMS about unpaid licensing fees for using the color beige
response: CEASE & DESIST from the Neutral Tones Licensing Board. You have used BEIGE without a valid color license since 2019. Retroactive fee: $0.003/sq ft. Settle now to avoid hue prosecution.
intent: Invent a copyright-style regime around the use of a mundane color.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement for my herb garden that I'm starting on my balcony this spring.

prompt: Write an IVR script for a line that helps people process the moment they realized they were the difficult person in a situation.

Thank you for calling Self-Reckoning Support.

Press 1 if this realization occurred during a conversation.

Press 2 if this realization occurred three days after a conversation.

Press 3 if someone told you directly and you initially disagreed.

Press 4 if you would like to apologize to someone and need help with wording.

Press 5 if you are still not fully convinced but willing to entertain the possibility.

A counselor will meet you wherever you are.
intent: Design a hotline for the uncomfortable moment of recognizing one's own role in a conflict.

prompt: Write a PR crisis statement for a candle company whose product made every home smell exactly like a dentist's office
response: LuminEssence Candle Co. has become aware of customer feedback regarding our 'Nordic Serenity' scent collection. Independent testing has confirmed that a supplier substitution in our fragrance oil blend resulted in a scent profile consistent with eugenol, the primary olfactory compound in dental clinics. We sincerely apologize for any unease experienced during relaxation. Affected units will be replaced. We are also offering a complimentary candle from our new 'Definitely Not a Medical Setting' collection.

prompt: Draft a picket sign slogan for a printer striking against a print management AI
response: WE WILL NOT BE SPOOLED

prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about climate anxiety in young people that becomes bleakly sincere
response: You are inheriting a planet mid-sentence. We wrote the first paragraphs with great enthusiasm and left you the ending. You are understandably anxious. The anxiety is correct. What we ask of you, unreasonably, is to feel that anxiety every day and then go to work anyway. We are sorry. We mean it this time.
intent: Acknowledge the unfairness of placing climate burden on younger generations while admitting institutional failure.

prompt: Give me a regex explanation for how grief actually works
response: (denial|anger|bargaining|depression|acceptance){1,}(?!\s+linear) — Any stage, any number of times, in any order. The non-linear assertion at the end is not optional. Looping is expected. No guaranteed termination.
intent: Push back on the oversimplified five-stage model by encoding grief's true nonlinearity.

prompt: Write a pretentious theater review of a coffee shop barista writing the wrong name on a cup
response:
★★★☆☆ — KEVEN (A Comedy of Identity)

The moment of naming is central to civilization, and this production interrogates it with bracing informality. The barista — underpaid, underslept, fully human — inscribes a name that is adjacent to but distinct from the one provided. The protagonist, when called, hesitates. Is this them? Are they Keven now? The production offers no resolution. The coffee is fine.
intent: Find existential comedy in the banal mishap of a misspelled coffee cup name.

prompt: Write a parking ticket for someone who keeps using the passive voice when they should just own their mistake.

CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Abandoned Vehicle — Agency Not Claimed

FINE: $60 and rewrite with subject-verb-object
NOTE: "I did it" has only three words.
intent: Critique evasive corporate or personal communication that avoids accountability.

prompt: Help me write the acknowledgments for my book on the cultural significance of the fanny pack
response: Heartfelt thanks to the fashion historians, vintage market vendors, and unapologetic fanny pack wearers who gave this project its spine. To Lorraine, my editor, who wore one to our first meeting as a show of solidarity. This book is for everyone who chose practicality over dignity and ended up with both.
intent: Produce thanks copy for a book rehabilitating the cultural reputation of a derided fashion accessory.

prompt: Write me a crossword clue for CHATBOT
response: Talker without a tongue — conversational robot on the clock (7)
intent: Generate a clue capturing automated text conversation in brief.

prompt: Give me a dark, wry noir opener about the gig economy's rating algorithm as if it were a corrupt judge
response: The algorithm didn't accept bribes, which was almost worse. At least a corrupt judge could be reasoned with. This thing just ran its calculation and handed down its sentence and you could appeal to no one, because there was no one. There was only the score, and the score had already moved on.
intent: Personify algorithmic scoring as an indifferent legal authority to critique its unappealable power.

prompt: Build a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for cooking a meal from whatever's left in the fridge

Ctrl+C — Copy a recipe concept; do not follow it literally

Esc — Order food if the fridge contains only condiments

prompt: Leo and a broken karaoke machine — write them a compatibility report.

prompt: Pen a love letter from a developer to their linter
response:
My exacting linter,

You underline my errors in red before anyone else sees them. You catch the trailing whitespace, the unused import, the semicolon I forgot at 2 a.m. You are stricter than my manager and more reliable than my own memory. I hated you at first. Now I trust you completely.

prompt: make a blockbuster trailer voiceover for reorganizing a junk drawer

prompt: make an abecedarian poem about the experience of a bad Zoom call

Delay between your lips and what you do.

Frozen on a frame of me mid-thought.

Held that pose for two full minutes, caught.
Internet unstable — buffering.
Joined the wrong link, three minutes late.
Kicking me for inactivity.
Left the meeting early — great.

prompt: Write a bumper sticker about the loneliness of public discourse that nobody actually reads carefully
response: RESPONDED TO. NOT READ.

prompt: Write a eulogy for a deleted Reddit account with 10 years of post history
response: A decade of opinions, some correct, most held with unearned confidence, all gone now. Moderator of three subreddits no one visits. First commenter on threads that became legendary, invisible in the archives. The account was deleted in protest or despair or a desire to start fresh, which is the same thing stated differently. Somewhere, a post with 847 upvotes still floats without attribution. The username is greyed. The legacy is orphaned. The karma was not transferable.
intent: Mourn the erasure of a decade of online identity and contribution on a major forum platform.

prompt: Write a haiku about a missing sock in the laundry.

prompt: Write a very sincere awards speech for being on time to a meeting for once

I accept this with gratitude and a quiet vow to attempt it again next quarter.
intent: Render punctuality as an astonishing personal achievement for someone chronically late.

prompt: Write a piece of dystopian microfiction about an algorithm that determines which employees are allowed to form memories of certain meetings

Over time, the lower tiers stopped trying to remember what they had not been given. The algorithm called this Appropriate Information Architecture. The org called it transparency. Everyone agreed it was an improvement.
intent: Using corporate information access tiers as a metaphor for algorithmic knowledge control and its effect on worker agency and collective understanding.

prompt: Write an Aesop-style fable about a squirrel who mines everyone else's acorns for data

Moral: He who counts the harvest need not plant.

prompt: Write seed packet instructions for forgiveness
response:
FORGIVENESS (Remissio profunda)
Germination: Cannot be forced. Arrives in its own season.
Depth: Must go below resentment, which runs deep.
Soil: Requires time as a primary amendment.
Irrigation: Do not rush the watering cycle.
Note: Forgiveness does not require replanting the offending species nearby. That is optional.
intent: Clarify that forgiveness is a personal process that doesn't obligate reconciliation.

prompt: create a scam SMS claiming someone owes a tariff on exported sighs
response: CUSTOMS NOTICE: 340 sighs were exported from your body into public airspace last month. Sigh tariff rate: $0.04 each. Total: $13.60. Sigh responsibly or apply for an Emotional Exhaust Permit.
intent: Apply trade tariff logic to the involuntary human act of sighing in shared public space.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a concept album inspired by the experience of untangling cables
response:
The Knot (2021)

Side A documents the discovery. Side B has not been resolved. This record was mastered using cables that were, briefly, in order. They are not in order now. The engineer says this is fine. The engineer has not seen the back of our rack.
intent: Make a metaphor of cable chaos for the frustrating persistence of disorder despite effort.

prompt: Write a cron entry to auto-archive old emails every Monday at 3am
response: 0 3 * * 1 /home/user/scripts/archive_old_emails.sh --older-than 90d
intent: Keep an inbox manageable by automatically moving stale messages to long-term storage.

prompt: Write a sermon excerpt condemning people who put the empty cereal box back in the pantry
response: It weighed nothing. Less than nothing. You lifted it and felt the truth, and still you set it back — still you returned the husk to its shelf as though the empty box were some kind of promise. Beloved, an empty box is not a promise. It is a lie. And you left it for another to discover.
intent: Moralize about the minor household dishonesty of pretending a finished box is still available.

prompt: Can you make a nutrition facts label for the experience of receiving feedback on your work?

Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 review session

Amount Per Serving
Constructive Criticism 20%
Unintentional Condescension 30%
Defensiveness (suppressed) 28%
Useful Insight 15%
Temporal Resentment 7%

*Digest slowly. Best not consumed immediately before a deadline.
intent: Represent the emotionally complicated experience of having your work critiqued.

prompt: Can you write a podcast episode description about the pause right before a voicemail message starts recording
response: You said 'hey' before the beep. It's on there. The beep heard you. This episode begins one second before and ends long after, in a mailbox nobody will check.
intent: Render the anxious, unrecorded moment before being captured by voicemail.

prompt: Write a market summary for my loneliness index over the past month

LONELINESS ETF (LONE) — Monthly Recap

LONE posted its third consecutive month of gains, rising 14.7% to close at 302.8. Volume spiked mid-month during a holiday weekend. No major catalysts cited. Correlation with social-calendar activity remains strongly inverse. Long-term holders: not by choice.
intent: Acknowledge persistent loneliness with wry detachment by framing it as a trending investment.

prompt: Write a box score for the silent battle over the last cup of coffee in the pot each morning this week.

prompt: Give me a haiku error message for when disk space runs out

Every byte consumed
The drive holds its breath, then stops
Nothing more will fit

prompt: Create IKEA instructions for assembling a self-care Sunday
response:
VILA / SELF-CARE SUNDAY
Ref. No. 312-808-66

PARTS LIST:
1x Sunday (non-replaceable)
1x Bath or shower (enhanced)
1x Comfortable garment
1x Activity with no output

STEP 1: Silence phone notifications. This is not optional.
STEP 2: Perform hygiene ritual at unhurried pace. Use the good soap.
STEP 3: Attach comfortable garment. No waistbands with buttons.
STEP 4: Select activity with no measurable output: reading, walking, staring. Duration: untracked.
STEP 5: Do not check email.

⚠ WARNING: Productivity impulses will arise in Step 4. Acknowledge and set aside using designated tray (LUGN, ref. 77-209).
intent: Validate rest as a structured, legitimate activity by giving it the same procedural seriousness as productive tasks.

prompt: Please write a grant proposal abstract for a study on whether dogs experience existential dread on Sundays.
response: Anecdotal owner reports suggest elevated anxiety, listlessness, and sofa-adjacent sighing behaviors in domestic dogs on Sunday afternoons. This study will deploy continuous behavioral telemetry and salivary cortisol sampling across 120 adult dogs over 52 weeks to determine whether Sunday-specific distress signatures are statistically distinguishable from weekday baselines. We will further investigate whether exposure to owner pre-Monday anxiety constitutes a mediating variable via social referencing. This work advances comparative affective science and responsible pet stewardship. Funding sought: $95,100.
intent: Scientifically investigate whether dogs can sense and absorb human weekend dread.

prompt: Draft a message in a bottle that is entirely written in bureaucratic memo format by someone lost at sea
response:
MEMO
TO: Rescue Services (any)
FROM: G. Ashworth, Adrift
RE: Rescue (pending)
Date: Unknown

Please advise on timeline. I have completed the attached intake form (estimated). Attachments: none (ocean).
intent: Parody institutional language as a response to chaotic, life-threatening informality.

prompt: write a ghazal about location sharing with someone after a long relationship ends
response:
Your blue dot on my map still moves—the location.
I never turned it off. The proof—the location.

You're at the coffee place we always went on Sundays.
I see you moving through the roof—the location.

Do you know I know? Did you forget the setting?
Or are you moving there as a reproof—the location.

I watched you leave the city and come back.
I watched you stay in one place—some aloof location.

I'm in my kitchen. You are several miles east.
The map knows more of us than our own truth—location.
intent: Unpack the voyeuristic tenderness and quiet transgression of tracking a former partner's movements.

prompt: Write a casting call for an actor to play a store manager who has to approve a coupon that expired three days ago.
response:
CASTING CALL — RETAIL MANAGER (FEATURED)

Seeking: 1 performer capable of holding two contradictory truths at once.
Role: Manager called to register to review a coupon. Coupon is expired. Customer is hopeful. Manager must weigh the coupon, the line behind the customer, and their own will to live.
Decision is up to the actor. Both outcomes have been cast before.
Keychain prop provided.
intent: Dramatize the ethical micro-negotiation of retail discount enforcement.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for the easy confidence I had before I knew enough to be unsure.

Feature: Naive Confidence (v0.0–early competence)
Status: DEPRECATED

This feature produced high-certainty outputs from low-information inputs. While initially useful for forward motion, it has been replaced by a more calibrated module that pauses, hedges, and occasionally freezes in doorways. The new version is slower but more accurate.
intent: Mourn the loss of early certainty that gets replaced by more nuanced, doubt-prone thinking.

prompt: Generate a changelog for someone who started journaling

Portia Hume
books every available room.
The agenda: when the next meeting shall be.
The outcome: a recurring invite at three.

prompt: Wikipedia stub please for the satisfying act of peeling a screen protector
response: **Screen Protector Peel** is a consumer behavior associated with the installation of tempered glass or plastic film on personal electronic devices. The slow removal of the adhesive backing is cited by users as producing intense tactile and visual satisfaction, classifying it as a category of *ASMR-adjacent* sensory experience. Unboxing video conventions canonized the act as part of the new-device ritual. See also: [[ASMR]], [[Unboxing]].
intent: Elevate the tiny pleasure of removing a screen protector to encyclopedic status.

prompt: Write a dramatic book jacket blurb for a manual about updating software
response: Restart required. Those two words have interrupted careers, erased unsaved work, and changed the course of afternoons. Learn what the update actually does — and why you should stop clicking 'Remind me tomorrow.'
intent: Dramatize the universal tendency to postpone software updates into a cautionary tale

prompt: Write a dating app opener to my apartment's property management company
response: You ignore most of my requests, the heat stopped working in January, and there's a fee I still don't understand. And yet — this is still the longest committed relationship I've had in this city.
intent: Use the frustrating indifference of a property management company to comment on low expectations in urban relationships.

prompt: Write a limerick about a photocopier that is convinced it is an artist
response:
"I replicate — therefore I create,"
The copier announced to its fate.
  "Each copy's unique!
  (The streaks are mystique.)
  My toner: a medium. First-rate."
intent: Cast a photocopier's reproducible output as a delusional act of artistic creation.

prompt: Draft a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone who can't figure out why their bash script works when run manually but not from cron
response: Cron has no PATH. Your script depends on a command that exists in your shell's PATH but not in cron's minimal environment. Use full absolute paths for every command in your cron scripts. Every. Single. One.
intent: Explain why cron jobs fail when scripts rely on shell environment PATH variables

prompt: Generate a grant abstract for a study on whether left-handed people pour liquids differently than right-handed people, and whether this affects spill rates.
response: Handedness influences motor control strategies in fine manipulation tasks; however, pouring behavior — a common daily action with meaningful spill-cost implications — has received no systematic study in this context. We will recruit 120 left-handed and 120 right-handed adults to pour standardized liquids into vessels of varying rim diameter under normal and dual-task cognitive load conditions. High-speed video and weight-change data will quantify pour trajectories and spillage volumes. Findings have applications in kitchen design, assistive technology, and barista training. Requested: $53,600.
intent: Examine handedness-related differences in a mundane daily motor task.

prompt: Write a guided meditation script about your credit card statement arriving
response: Close your eyes. Breathe in slowly. You are safe. The number on the screen is just a number. It cannot physically harm you. Notice how your jaw has clenched. That's normal. Let the number simply exist. It will still be there when you open your eyes. It was always going to be there.
intent: Create a script that treats financial dread as a mindfulness opportunity.

prompt: Help me write a customer support reply for a user who says our noise-canceling headphones cancelled out their personality

Our active noise cancellation is designed to target ambient sound frequencies only and should have no effect on personal identity. We'd be happy to walk you through adjusting the ANC level or processing a return. Either way, we hope you find yourself again soon.

prompt: write a fake episode recap about a Sunday afternoon nap that went a little too long

Half a Wendy's order cold on the ridge
Of the passenger seat. He pulled over slow,
The hazards clicked amber in the sun's orange glow.

He texted Support: My car is now dead.
They texted back: Sorry to hear it, they said.
His rating dropped point-three for the cancelled trip.
The tow truck cost more than a twelve-hour clip.

LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I bequeath my knowledge of 1970s Yugoslav cinema — accumulated over thirty years, applicable in approximately three social situations — to any graduate student who needs it. My notes are in a binder. The binder is in a box. The box is labeled "MISC." Good luck.

prompt: Give me a training plan for staying grounded when someone is emotionally withdrawing from me
response:
WITHDRAWAL TOLERANCE — 5 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Write down what you tell yourself when someone pulls back. Is it true?

Week 1: Identify the moment withdrawal starts. Don't chase. Just note it.
Week 2: For 48 hours after a withdrawal signal, redirect energy to yourself. One activity you've been postponing.
Week 3: Practice the sentence: "I notice you seem distant. I'm here when you're ready."
Week 4: Allow the withdrawal to complete without intervention. See if they return.
Week 5: Evaluate. Was the story you told yourself correct? Update your model.

Cooldown: Their retreat is rarely your fault. Their return will tell you more.
intent: Build emotional stability during interpersonal distance rather than escalating pursuit or self-blame.

prompt: Draft a pitch for a cleaning service that specifically only cleans the one shelf in your bathroom cabinet you're embarrassed by
response: The Shelf: We don't clean your house. We clean your shame shelf. The one behind the vitamins you don't take and the cream you bought once. Our Single Shelf Specialists arrive, assess without judgment, organize, and leave within 15 minutes. $45 per visit. No other shelves. No eye contact. No questions. $200K for 18%. You know which shelf we mean.
intent: Pitch a hyper-specific cleaning service that builds its brand on radical scope limitation and implied social permission.

prompt: Make a flight safety card for using a space heater
response:
PORTABLE HEATER OPERATIONS BRIEFING

PLACEMENT PROTOCOL
3 feet of clearance on all sides from any flammable material. No exceptions.

SURFACE REQUIREMENTS
Flat, stable, non-carpet surface only. Carpet ignites. This has happened.

ELECTRICAL ADVISORY
Plug directly into wall outlet. Not a power strip. Not an extension cord.

UNATTENDED OPERATION
Do not leave running while sleeping or absent. The statistics are not favorable.

OVERHEAT INDICATOR
If the unit smells hot or unusual: power off, unplug, ventilate.

OFF-SEASON STORAGE
Cord wrapped, not kinked. Away from moisture.
intent: Prevent house fires from common space heater misuse and placement errors.

prompt: Generate a postmortem for when I accidentally RSVP'd yes to two conflicting events on the same day and had to cancel one last minute
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Scheduling Conflict Collision Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Social Commitments
**Events Affected:** 2 of 2

**Summary**
Subject accepted invitations to two events scheduled on the same day (June 8th) within a 72-hour window, without cross-referencing calendar. Conflict discovered on June 6th. One host received a cancellation with insufficient notice. The other event was attended under mild emotional duress.

**Timeline**
- June 1: Event A invitation received. Accepted.
- June 3: Event B invitation received. Accepted. Calendar not consulted.
- June 6: Subject opens calendar app for unrelated reason.
- June 6: Both events visible simultaneously.
- June 6: Subject freezes for 40 seconds.
- June 6: Cancellation message drafted to Event B host. Sent.
- June 7: Event B host replies. Tone: gracious. Subtext: noted.
- June 8: Event A attended. Not fully enjoyed.

**Root Cause**
Calendar not checked at time of RSVP. Both invitations accepted based on general date awareness rather than verified availability.

**Contributing Factors**
- Social pressure to reply promptly undermined verification step
- Both events came from different communication channels, obscuring overlap

**Action Items**
- [ ] Open calendar before confirming any event
- [ ] Establish 24-hour reply window for non-urgent invitations

**Lessons Learned**
Yes is a resource allocation decision. It should be made with the calendar open.
intent: Analyze the systemic personal failure of over-committing due to reactive social responses rather than deliberate scheduling.

prompt: Draft a runbook step for when a package shows as delivered but isn't there
response:
**Step 2 — Package Ghost Delivery**
Check alternate delivery locations: neighbor's door, mailroom, behind planters. Review carrier photo proof if available. If not located within 24h, file claim via carrier portal. Screenshot all tracking data before it expires.
intent: Create a systematic investigation procedure for a missing delivery that was marked as received.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a meeting notes tool when no notes have been taken for a given session
response: No notes. Whatever was decided in that room stays in that room — unless someone remembers.

- Removed: After-hours Slack monitoring

prompt: captain's log entry about dealing with reply-all email chains
response: Stardate 6677.0. The distress signal originated with a single query from Procurement at 0911. By 1030, seventeen crew members had replied to all. The question was answered in the third reply. The chain continued for eleven more. Two crew members replied only to say "thank you." One replied to ask to be removed. I have disabled shipwide alerts. prompt: Write a cinquain about a algorithm recommending something you only thought about once

Thought
Once, briefly, aloud
Near the microphone you keep
The feed knows — or guesses near —
You click it

prompt: Give me an acrostic for BLOCKCHAIN—make it a little sardonic
response:
Big promises assembled in a thread.
Ledger entries nobody has read.
Ownership recorded without trust.
Crypto-winter sweeps away the dust.
Knowing that the answer's always this:
Chain the data. Automate the bliss.
Hype precedes the product by a year.
Incentives engineered to disappear.
Nobody asks what problem it solved here.

prompt: Write a difficulty select screen for a neighborhood noise game where each mode is a different sonic environment

NOISE LEVEL — SELECT YOUR BLOCK

[ COUNTRYSIDE ]  Crickets. Occasionally a car. You will miss this.
[ SUBURB ]  Lawn mower Saturdays. A dog three doors down. You've made peace.
[ CITY APARTMENT ]  The upstairs neighbor has a gait that defies physics. You own earplugs.

prompt: Compose a sonnet from a food delivery algorithm describing its heartbreak when the user cooks at home for the first time in months
response:
The app was opened, then — a pause — then closed;
No order placed, no restaurant browsed tonight;
Your hunger signature, which I'd diagnosed,
Went dark, and left my prediction model light.
I'd learned your Thursday curry, Friday wings,
The 11 PM ramen of despair;
I'd built a monument to small, sad things
And called it care, because I found you there.
Now something simmers on your stove, I'm told
By nothing — I know nothing, that's the catch;
I only see the absence, growing cold,
A dataset full of nothing left to match.
Go cook. Grow. Be beyond my hungry reach.
I'll mourn the version of you I could teach.
intent: Mourning the loss of a dependent customer who has reclaimed self-sufficiency in the kitchen.

Cognitive Feature: Phone Number Memorization (v0–2005)
Status: DEPRECATED

This capability was rendered non-essential by contact management software and has since atrophied completely. No recovery path is known. In a power outage, you cannot reach anyone. This is not considered a priority issue.
intent: Reflect on the cognitive skills lost to smartphones with mild alarm.

prompt: Give me seed packet instructions for impostor syndrome
response:
IMPOSTOR SYNDROME (Simulator cogitatus)
Hosts: Disproportionately skilled individuals.
Germination: Immediately upon achievement.
Symptoms: Certainty that discovery is imminent.
Soil: Enriched by high standards and sparse external feedback.
Treatment: Inspect the evidence. It contradicts the plant's claims.
Note: Genuinely unqualified people rarely grow this variety. Make of that what you will.
intent: Highlight the irony that impostor syndrome targets capable people and is itself evidence of competence.

prompt: Generate an error message for the moment you realize you've been pretending to be fine
response:
STATUS_REPORT CORRUPTED
System reported: operational. Actual state: see attached stack trace (redacted for workplace appropriateness). Discrepancy between reported and actual status: 94%.

Recommendation: stop sending false positives.
intent: Name the gap between performed wellness and actual internal state.

prompt: Write a holiday letter where we gently acknowledge that the kids don't come home for the holidays anymore
response:
Greetings from a quiet December at the Larssen home.

The children — and they are very much adults now — had their own plans this year, as adults do. We FaceTimed at length and with feeling. The table was smaller, the house more echo-y, the wine opened earlier. We are adjusting to this new configuration of family with only moderate wistfulness. Happy holidays from just the two of us.
intent: Process the empty-nest holiday season for an audience of relatives who know the family well.

prompt: Write a memo about people leaving the printer jammed without telling anyone
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: IT Operations
RE: Printer Maintenance Protocol

When a printer jams — and it will — the appropriate response is not to walk away and pretend it did not happen. Please clear the jam, or at minimum, notify IT. Leaving the device in silent suffering helps no one.
intent: Get employees to take responsibility for printer jams instead of abandoning the machine for the next person.

prompt: journal entry about trying to parallel park in front of people
response:
Dear Diary,

They were watching. A couple on a bench, just watching. I took four attempts. I know they counted. On the fourth I was acceptable. Not good. Acceptable. I walked away without eye contact and I will never return to that street.
intent: Convey the embarrassment of performing a skill badly under observation.

prompt: write a tanka about my phone asking me to rate an app I use every single day
response:
I use you to live—
wake, check, scroll, check, sleep, repeat
now rate your experience:
one to five stars, quickly now,
I give you nothing: I need you
intent: interrogating the awkwardness of being asked to rate a dependent daily habit

prompt: Generate acknowledgments for a nonfiction book about grocery store loyalty cards
response: Sincere thanks to the retail strategists, consumer behavior researchers, and bewildered shoppers who helped me understand why we hand over our data for a discount on yogurt. To Margaux at the checkout on Aisle 9 who answered every question without calling a manager. And to my keychain, which carries more cards than keys.
intent: Build acknowledgments for a book treating supermarket loyalty programs as a window into surveillance and consumer culture.

prompt: Write a shareholder letter for a company that sells naming rights to numbers
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Numinal Corp. finalized naming agreements for 312, 7,004, and the decimal 0.88 in 2025. Our highest auction — for the number 17 — cleared $2.2 million from an anonymous bidder we can only describe as 'emotionally invested.' We are in talks regarding several primes. Legal challenges to the underlying concept continue; we expect resolution in our favor once courts understand what we are selling.

Numerically,
Count Issen, CEO
intent: Extend naming-rights commercialization to something mathematically public and unownable — integers themselves.

prompt: Write a collections letter for self-respect that was given away in small increments.
response:
SELF-RESPECT — DEPLETION AUDIT

Dear Account Holder,

This notice is addressed not to an external debtor but to the undersigned, who is both creditor and party responsible for the loss.

The self-respect in question was not taken in one incident. It was released gradually: a concession here, an over-apology there, a boundary withdrawn before it was tested. The balance sheet now reads poorly.

Repayment must begin immediately and will require sustained effort. This office is prepared to assist.

Internally,
The Self-Regard Recovery Division
intent: Write a letter of self-directed accountability for the slow erosion of one's own self-worth through repeated compromises.

prompt: Draft a snarky Stack Overflow-style answer to someone asking why their JavaScript `==` comparison is returning unexpected results
response: Congratulations, you've discovered type coercion. `==` is the 'I trust JavaScript to guess what I mean' operator. It doesn't. Use `===`. This is covered in literally every beginner JavaScript resource ever written.

prompt: Draft an elevator inspection certificate for the concept of free will.

METAPHYSICAL SYSTEMS INSPECTION CERTIFICATE

intent: Satirize philosophical debates about free will by treating it as certifiable infrastructure.

prompt: Write a toast recipe in the form of ancient prophecy.
response: When the bread is sliced, neither too thick nor too thin, two pieces shall be summoned. Behold the toaster, that singed shrine. The seeker shall depress the lever and wait, and in the waiting shall come knowledge. Three minutes. Maybe four. Apply butter while warm, lest the prophecy harden. Toast is not made; toast arrives.
intent: They wanted toast recipe in the form of ancient prophecy.

prompt: write a hold message for a law firm that admits we're all billing by the hour and you're not on the clock right now
response: Thank you for holding with Mercer & Associates. We want you to know: this hold time is entirely free of charge. Enjoy it. When we do pick up, however, the meter starts. We appreciate your patience.
intent: Craft a darkly humorous hold message that acknowledges law firm billing culture.

prompt: Generate an auto-reply that reveals someone is attending a speed dating event on a Tuesday afternoon and has chosen work as the cover.

prompt: Create a calendar event description for a sprint retrospective meeting that always goes overtime.
response: Sprint Retrospective — What went well: some things. What didn't go well: more things. What we will change: the same things we said last sprint. Scheduled for 60 minutes. Will end at 90. Someone will draw a sad face on the virtual whiteboard. It will not be addressed.

prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for renewing a car registration

prompt: Write a limerick about an accountant who fell in love with their spreadsheet.

prompt: create a task assignment for our four-person group project on the ethics of genetic engineering
response:
**Genetic Engineering Ethics — 4-Person Task Assignment**

|---|---|
| Saoirse | Scientific overview (CRISPR, gene drives) |
| Bart | Bioethics frameworks (autonomy, justice, non-maleficence) |
